input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **system under study** in the provided documents pertains to **cyber-biophysical systems**, specifically focusing on **autonomous vehicles with Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems** implemented on a **1/10-scale model**. The physical twin system is a **scaled-down autonomous vehicle** designed for **real-time cyber-physical integration**, where **sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, cameras, radar)** and **control algorithms** interact with a **physical prototype** to enable **dynamic behavior replication**. The operational domain is **automotive cyber-physical systems**, emphasizing **real-time adaptability, edge computing, and remote management** for **safety-critical applications**. Key characteristics include:
  - **Scaled-down physical model** (1/10 scale) for controlled experimentation.
  - **Real-time sensor fusion** (LiDAR, cameras, radar) for environmental perception.
  - **Embedded edge computing** (NVIDIA Jetson hardware) for low-latency processing.
  - **Hybrid simulation-physical testing** to validate **ACC algorithms** and **collision avoidance**.
  - **Cyber-physical coupling** via **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)** for seamless data exchange between physical and digital components.
  - **Dynamic adaptability** to simulate **real-world driving scenarios** (e.g., traffic, obstacles) in a **scalable, reproducible environment**.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT) services** provided in the documents are categorized into **monitoring, optimization, prediction, control, and visualization**, tailored for **engineers, operators, and decision-makers** across automotive, manufacturing, aerospace, and battery domains. Key services include:

1. **Monitoring (Documents [7], [8], [9], [10], [12], [14], [17])**:
   - **Data Collection**: Real-time acquisition of sensor data (e.g., LiDAR, depth cameras, thermal sensors) via edge devices (e.g., NVIDIA Jetson).
   - **Viewing and Reporting**: Generation of dashboards and reports for performance metrics (e.g., vehicle trajectory, manufacturing yield) using SQL databases (Document [14]).
   - **Anomaly Detection**: Basic fault detection in manufacturing processes (ISO 23247-1:2021) and battery degradation (Document [17]).
   - **Target Users**: Plant managers, maintenance technicians, and quality assurance teams.

2. **Optimization (Documents [7], [9], [10], [14])**:
   - **Process Optimization**: Adjustment of manufacturing parameters (e.g., robotic arm trajectories, assembly line speeds) via DT simulations.
   - **Resource Allocation**: Dynamic scheduling of edge computing resources (e.g., Jetson Orin Nano) for load balancing (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**: Production engineers and supply chain planners.

3. **Prediction (Documents [7], [9], [16], [17])**:
   - **Failure Prediction**: Prognostic health management (PHM) for batteries (Document [17]) and aerospace components (Document [16]).
   - **Trajectory Prediction**: Collision avoidance algorithms for satellites (Document [16]) and autonomous vehicles (Document [7]).
   - **Lifespan Forecasting**: Estimation of remaining useful life (RUL) for manufacturing equipment and vehicle components.
   - **Target Users**: Predictive maintenance teams and aerospace mission planners.

4. **Control (Documents [7], [8], [14])**:
   - **Real-Time Control**: Closed-loop adjustments for autonomous vehicles (e.g., adaptive cruise control, ACC) via DT simulations (Document [8]).
   - **Edge-Based Control**: Offloading computational tasks (e.g., perception models) to edge devices (NVIDIA Jetson) to reduce latency (Document [14]).
   - **Target Users**: Vehicle control systems engineers and autonomous driving developers.

5. **Visualization (Documents [7], [10], [12], [14])**:
   - **3D Simulation**: Interactive visualizations of physical systems (e.g., vehicle dynamics, manufacturing layouts) using MATLAB/Simulink (Document [14]).
   - **Digital Shadow**: Real-time mirroring of physical states (e.g., battery SoH, satellite orbits) for intuitive monitoring.
   - **Target Users**: Engineers, operators, and end-users requiring intuitive insights.

**Service Capabilities**:
   - **Interoperability**: Compliance with ISO 23247-1:2021 ensures seamless integration with legacy and modern systems.
   - **Scalability**: Support for both small-scale (e.g., 1/10 autonomous vehicles) and large-scale (e.g., factory-wide) deployments.
   - **Adaptability**: Modular design allows customization for specific domains (e.g., aerospace collision avoidance vs. battery thermal management).
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **tooling and enablers** for Digital Twin systems in the provided documents include **software platforms, simulation engines, databases, and edge/cloud technologies**, with a focus on **automotive, manufacturing, and aerospace applications**. Key components are:

1. **Software Platforms (Documents [7], [8], [12], [14], [15])**:
   - **ROS2 (Robot Operating System 2)**: Enables real-time communication between sensors, actuators, and simulation engines for autonomous vehicles and manufacturing robots (Document [7], [14]).
   - **MATLAB/Simulink**: Used for modeling, simulation, and code generation of cyber-physical systems (e.g., vehicle dynamics, control algorithms) (Document [14], [15]).
   - **Custom Frameworks**: Edge computing-based DT frameworks aligned with ISO 23247 (Document [14]), supporting modular and scalable architectures.

2. **Simulation Engines (Documents [7], [10], [12], [15])**:
   - **High-Fidelity Simulators**: Digital twins leverage **physics-based simulators** (e.g., for vehicle crash testing, manufacturing process validation) to replicate real-world behavior.
   - **AI/ML Integration**: Simulation engines incorporate **deep learning models** for perception tasks (e.g., object detection in autonomous vehicles) (Document [15]).
   - **Co-Simulation Tools**: Integration with **co-simulation platforms** (e.g., for coupling multi-domain models like thermal, electrical, and mechanical systems).

3. **Databases (Documents [7], [14])**:
   - **SQL Databases**: Store sensor data, simulation results, and DT metadata for real-time querying and analysis (Document [14]).
   - **NoSQL/Time-Series Databases**: Implied for handling high-velocity data streams from IoT sensors (e.g., LiDAR, depth cameras).

4. **Edge Computing Technologies (Documents [7], [8], [14])**:
   - **NVIDIA Jetson (Orin Nano)**: Embedded AI accelerator for edge-based perception and control tasks (e.g., autonomous vehicles, Document [14]).
   - **TensorRT**: Optimized runtime for deep learning inference on Jetson devices (implied in [14] for perception models).
   - **Edge-Cloud Hybrid**: Offloading non-critical computations to cloud servers while keeping latency-sensitive tasks on edge (Document [8]).

5. **Specialized Technologies (Documents [7], [12], [16])**:
   - **Cybersecurity Protocols**: TLS encryption and secure communication channels for DT data (implied in [12] for IoT-based DTs).
   - **Standardized Interfaces**: Adherence to **ISO 23247-1:2021** for DT architecture, ensuring interoperability between tools (e.g., ROS2, MATLAB/Simulink).
   - **Aerospace-Specific Tools**: Custom DT frameworks for satellite collision avoidance (Document [16]), integrating with **AIAA standards**.

6. **Development Tools (Documents [7], [12], [15])**:
   - **Model-Driven Engineering (MDE)**: Use of **UML/SysML** for DT modeling and ontology development (Document [7]).
   - **AI Simulation Tools**: Frameworks for integrating AI models (e.g., reinforcement learning) into DT simulations (Document [15]).
   - **Version Control**: Git-based collaboration for DT model development (implied in [12]).

**Technological Stack Summary**:
   - **Hardware**: NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, IoT sensors (LiDAR, depth cameras).
   - **Software**: ROS2, MATLAB/Simulink, SQL databases, TensorRT.
   - **Standards**: ISO 23247-1:2021, ISO/IEC 30173:2023.
   - **Architecture**: Edge-cloud hybrid, modular, and scalable.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system enforces a **100 ms clock synchronization accuracy** between physical and digital components to enable computation offloading and coordination between devices. This temporal constraint is mandated due to hardware limitations and aligns with stricter vehicular application standards. The system does not explicitly detail service-specific time scales or latency requirements beyond this synchronization threshold, though the **ROS2-based architecture** inherently supports real-time communication with configurable QoS policies (e.g., latency, jitter) for inter-node and inter-FE (Functional Entity) interactions. Temporal granularity is implicitly governed by the **entity-based decomposition** (Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, User Entity), where each entity’s ROS2 package implements functionality with hardware-dependent execution cycles (e.g., sensor data pre-processing, actuation, or 3D visualization updates).
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system follows a **decentralized, entity-based hierarchical structure** with three primary **Functional Entities (FEs)**—*Physical Twin Entity*, *Digital Twin Entity*, and *User Entity*—each deployed on **distinct hardware components**. This design diverges from ISO 23247 by consolidating resource-specific and general automotive functionalities (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*) within the *Physical Twin Entity*, which contrasts with the standard’s *OME (Object Management Entity)* separation. The system does not explicitly mention **multiple Digital Twin instances** or **centralized coordination mechanisms**, though the **ROS2 package-based deployment** (one package per FE) enables modular scalability. Responsibilities are scoped per entity: the *User Entity* handles *3D Visualization* and *Control Panel* (split from the standard *User Interface FE*), while the *Digital Twin Entity* manages data management and control mechanisms. The *Physical Twin Entity* bridges physical resources (sensors/actuators) and general automotive logic, implying a **single-instance deployment per hardware component** with no explicit redundancy or replication mechanisms described.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system adopts an **ISO 23247 RA (Reference Architecture)-inspired orchestration** with **ROS2-based microservices** as the core integration pattern. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an **individual ROS2 package**, containing one or more ROS2 nodes that encapsulate modular functionalities (e.g., data pre-processing, actuation, or visualization). The architecture leverages **entity-based decomposition** to map software components to hardware, with **cross-entity communication** facilitated via ROS2 topics/services and QoS policies. Resource management is implicit within the *Physical Twin Entity*, which hosts both **resource-specific** (e.g., sensor/actuator interfaces) and **general automotive functionalities** (e.g., *Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking*). System-wide coordination relies on **ROS2’s publish-subscribe model**, where entities exchange data (e.g., sensor streams, control commands) with **100 ms synchronization constraints**. The design introduces **substantial deviations from ISO 23247** by merging functionalities (e.g., splitting *User Interface FE* into visualization/control panel) and omitting the *Data Storage* FE, which lacks a direct FE alignment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247** standard, which defines an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where the **Digital Twin Entity** represents the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**—such as equipment, facilities, products, or processes—using computational models and data. The system incorporates **geometric, behavioral, and analytical models** to simulate physical attributes and operational behaviors of OMEs. Data sources are managed via the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, which interfaces with physical sensors and actuators to collect real-time operational data. The **Functional Entities (FEs)** within each entity handle specific tasks, such as data transformation (e.g., converting raw sensor data into human-interpretable formats) or interfacing with users. While the standard does not prescribe implementation methods, it implies compatibility with **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** for data exchange and interoperability. Model relationships are established through the **Digital Twin Entity**, which maintains consistency between physical and digital representations via bidirectional data flows. Data management approaches are not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, but the architecture suggests centralized coordination of data collection, processing, and dissemination among entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components:
The system incorporates **physical actuators** integrated within the **chassis control layer**, including:
  - **Vedder Electronic Speed Controller** for motor voltage command generation via an **Actuation Functional Entity (FE)**.
  - **Steering Servo Actuation** and **Motor Actuation** (as per **Table I**), converting high-level commands into precise physical movements.
  Actuation ranges and limitations are inferred from the **chassis control system**, which regulates **steering and speed** via low-level control mechanisms. **Safety constraints** are implied but not explicitly detailed, though the system architecture suggests hierarchical control with **Functional Entities** managing actuation logic. Remote control interfaces are not specified, but the **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** (ISO 23247) likely facilitates remote actuation via standardized protocols (e.g., OPC UA).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The system emphasizes **high-fidelity computational reflections** of physical elements, though explicit **validation and verification (V&V) methods** are not detailed. **Uncertainty sources** may arise from sensor noise (e.g., LiDAR/depth camera inaccuracies) and model discrepancies, but quantification methods are not described. **Error detection and correction mechanisms** are inferred to be embedded within **Functional Entities** (e.g., sensor data preprocessing), but specific algorithms (e.g., Kalman filtering, outlier rejection) are not mentioned. **Quality assurance (QA)** processes are implied via **clock synchronization** for data integrity (ISO 23247) but lack formalized uncertainty propagation or error correction frameworks. The standard does not mandate statistical validation or model calibration procedures.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in this Digital Twin System (DTS) involves the acquisition, preprocessing, and integration of sensor data from the physical twin entity (e.g., vehicle) into the digital twin entity. Key data types include **raw LiDAR point clouds**, **motor actuation signals**, **steering servo inputs**, and **vehicle state data** (e.g., speed, position). The **Data Pre-Processing Functional Entity (FE)** processes raw LiDAR data through **clustering (DBSCAN algorithm)** and **tracking (Kalman filter)** to generate structured object tracks and ego vehicle state. These processed data streams are then serialized and aligned via the **Data Translation FE**, which performs unit conversions and message type alignment for compatibility across the system. Data flows are transmitted over a **Wi-Fi network** using **UDP-based communication** (as implied by the truncated text 'using U' in the document). No explicit transmission frequencies are specified, but the system relies on real-time processing for **3D visualization** and **data monitoring**, as evidenced by the requirement to display vehicle state and object tracks in real-time (Fig. 3a). Data quality assurance is implicitly ensured through preprocessing (e.g., clustering/tracking) and the integration of processed data into a **SQL database** for storage and reporting. No explicit event triggers or validation mechanisms for raw data are mentioned beyond the preprocessing steps.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction:
The **virtual-to-physical interaction** in this DTS involves **control commands** generated by the **Controlling FE (ACC)** and transmitted to the **Actuation FE** for execution on physical actuators. The **command types** include **desired motor voltage commands** derived from speed control algorithms, which are converted into **actuation signals** for the motor and steering servo. The **control message format** is serialized and translated via the **Data Translation FE**, ensuring compatibility with the physical system’s input requirements. **Validation mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the system relies on the **Data Translation FE** for unit conversions and message alignment, implying implicit validation.

Feedback systems are not explicitly described, though the **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)** likely uses real-time data from the **Data Monitoring** service (e.g., vehicle state, object tracks) to adjust commands dynamically. **Conflict resolution** is not mentioned, but the system’s reliance on **ROS2** for intra-system communication suggests centralized coordination. **Command execution confirmation** is implied through the closed-loop nature of the actuation pipeline, where the **Actuation FE** processes translated commands into physical signals. No explicit latency targets or authentication protocols are documented beyond the system’s reliance on UDP/Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** of this DTS is designed for low-latency communication between entities, with a **Wi-Fi network** serving as the primary transport layer for inter-entity communication. Intra-entity communication (e.g., between software components) occurs via **ROS2**, a real-time middleware enabling structured data exchange. The system explicitly states a **latency requirement of 'at most 100 ms'** (R7), critical for real-time applications like autonomous driving. No explicit bandwidth requirements or redundancy mechanisms (e.g., failover protocols) are mentioned. Security protocols for network communication are **not in Document**. The architecture relies on **UDP-based communication** (inferred from the truncated text 'using U'), which is suitable for time-sensitive applications but lacks built-in reliability guarantees. The Data Translation FE ensures cross-component compatibility by serializing data streams and aligning message types, but no details on protocol stack specifics (e.g., TCP/UDP layers) beyond the transport layer are provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment:
The **deployment infrastructure** for this DTS is hosted on **NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0**-enabled hardware, leveraging **ROS2** for real-time operation. The system is implemented using **Python 3.8** and **C++**, with no explicit mention of containerization (e.g., Docker) or real-time operating systems (e.g., QNX). **Computational requirements** are implied by the use of **GPU-accelerated processing** (e.g., for point cloud clustering and 3D visualization) and **Kalman filtering**, but specific GPU models or CPU specifications are not provided. **Storage requirements** are not detailed beyond the need for **data storage** (e.g., raw LiDAR point clouds, processed tracks, and reports), which is likely managed by the **Data Storage** component.

**Scalability mechanisms** are not described, though the modular design (e.g., separate FEs for preprocessing, visualization, and control) suggests potential for horizontal scaling. The system supports **on-premise deployment** (given the Jetpack 5.0 hardware context) but does not mention cloud or edge deployment models. **Availability/reliability assurance** is not explicitly addressed, though the real-time constraints (e.g., 100 ms latency) imply critical system operations with implicit redundancy assumptions (e.g., ROS2’s fault tolerance).
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages:
The Digital Twin lifecycle as defined in **ISO 23247** follows an **entity-based reference architecture (RA)** where the lifecycle is implicitly structured through the interaction of four core entities: the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)**, **Data Collection and Device Control Entity**, **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**, and **User Entity**. While the standard does not explicitly enumerate temporal phases, the lifecycle can be inferred as follows:

  1. **Conceptualization & Design Phase**:
     - The **OME** (e.g., equipment, facilities, or processes) is identified and defined as an observable physical or operational element in manufacturing.
     - The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** is designed to digitally represent the OME, with **Functional Entities (FEs)** assigned to specific tasks (e.g., data transformation, user interface rendering, or OME actuation).
     - The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** is configured to interface with the OME for real-time data acquisition and control signals. The standard does not prescribe implementation details for FEs, allowing flexibility (e.g., OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) could be used for interoperability).
     - **Transition Management**: The alignment between the OME and DTE occurs during this phase, ensuring the DTE accurately mirrors the OME’s physical and operational characteristics.

  2. **Deployment Phase**:
     - The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** is deployed to collect sensor data and execute control commands for the OME.
     - The **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** is instantiated to provide a real-time digital replica of the OME, leveraging FEs for data processing, visualization, and user interaction.
     - The **User Entity** is integrated to enable monitoring, diagnostics, and control via human-machine interfaces (HMIs) or automated systems.
     - **Lifecycle Governance**: The standard does not specify governance frameworks, but the entity-based RA implies modular accountability for each entity’s functionality.

  3. **Operation & Evolution Phase**:
     - The DTE operates in parallel with the OME, continuously updating its state based on real-time data from the Data Collection Entity.
     - The **User Entity** facilitates decision-making through insights derived from the DTE (e.g., predictive maintenance, performance optimization).
     - **Evolution Management**: While not explicitly detailed, the modular design of FEs suggests incremental updates to the DTE (e.g., adding new analytics modules or refining data processing pipelines) without disrupting the overall architecture. The standard emphasizes adaptability but does not mandate version control or formal evolution strategies.

  The lifecycle governance is implicitly tied to the **ISO 23247 RA**, which mandates clear separation of concerns among entities and encourages interoperability through unspecified protocols (e.g., OPC UA).
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution:
The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin (DT) evolution** in the context of **ISO 23247** adhere to an **entity-centric methodology** where the **Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)** is mapped to its corresponding **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)**. The process is structured as follows:

  - **Engineering Methodology**:
    The DT is developed by decomposing the OME into **Functional Entities (FEs)**, each responsible for a specific task (e.g., data acquisition, transformation, user interaction, or OME actuation). The standard does not prescribe implementation guidelines for FEs, allowing flexibility in technology selection (e.g., OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA) for communication). The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** acts as the bridge between the physical OME and the DTE, handling bidirectional data flow (sensor inputs and control outputs).

  - **Quality Assurance (QA) and Testing**:
    Not explicitly detailed in the standard. The **ISO 23247 RA** focuses on architectural design rather than QA protocols, leaving validation methodologies unspecified. However, the modular entity structure implies that each FE could undergo individual testing (e.g., unit testing for data transformation logic) before integration into the DTE.

  - **Version Control and Evolution**:
    The standard does not mandate version control mechanisms or formal evolution strategies. However, the modular design of FEs suggests that incremental updates to the DTE (e.g., adding new analytics modules or refining data processing pipelines) can occur without disrupting the overall architecture. Evolution is implied to be managed through iterative refinement of FEs, though no specific tools or methodologies are prescribed.

  - **Development Phases**:
    The lifecycle implicitly includes phases where the DTE is **designed** (conceptualization), **deployed** (integration with OME and user interfaces), and **operated** (real-time updates). Transition between phases relies on the alignment of entities, with the **User Entity** providing interfaces for monitoring and control. The standard emphasizes adaptability but does not detail governance or versioning strategies beyond the entity-based RA.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making:
The **Digital Twin** in the **ISO 23247:2021** framework enables **automated and user-driven decision support** through its **entity-based architecture**, particularly via the **Digital Twin Entity (DTE)** and **User Entity**. Key capabilities include:

1. **Insights Generation**:
   - **Real-Time Analytics**: The **Data Collection and Device Control Entity** streams OME data to the **DTE**, enabling **time-series analysis** (e.g., equipment performance monitoring) and **predictive modeling** (implied by ACC system computations).
   - **Computation Offloading**: The DTE hosts **automated decision logic** (e.g., **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems**), where it:
     - Determines the **lead vehicle’s position** (via sensor data).
     - Computes **desired speed** based on vehicle dynamics.
     - Generates **control signals** for the OME (e.g., braking/throttling).
   - **Human-Interpretable Outputs**: The **User Entity** transforms raw data into **visual dashboards** or alerts, enabling operators to make **context-aware decisions**.

2. **Decision-Making Processes**:
   - **Automated Support**: The **DTE’s ACC system** exemplifies **rule-based decision automation**, where computations are executed independently of human input. Confidence measures are implied by the **accuracy of lead vehicle detection** and **speed calculations**, though formal validation metrics (e.g., RMSE) are not specified.
   - **User Interaction**: The **User Entity** provides **interactive interfaces** (e.g., control panels) for manual overrides or parameter adjustments, blending **automation with human judgment**.

3. **Confidence Measures**:
   - The standard does not detail **formal confidence metrics** (e.g., Bayesian inference, uncertainty quantification) but assumes **data fidelity** is maintained through **real-time synchronization** between OMEs and DTEs. The **ACC system’s performance** indirectly serves as a confidence indicator, though no thresholds or validation protocols are defined.

*Note*: While **automated decision support** (e.g., ACC logic) is explicitly described, **confidence measures** and **user-driven decision frameworks** are inferred rather than explicitly outlined.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: {'description': 'The Digital Twin system adheres to the **ISO 23247:2021 standard**, which defines a **reference architecture for manufacturing Digital Twins** across four parts: Overview, Reference Architecture, Digital Representation, and Information Exchange. **Relevant industry standards** include the **entity-based RA** (Part 2), where **Observable Manufacturing Elements (OMEs)** are mapped to **Digital Twin Entities** via **Data Collection and Device Control Entities**. The standard does not mandate specific **certification requirements** or **compliance frameworks**, but it enforces **interoperability** through modular **Functional Entities (FEs)**. While **OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA)** is mentioned as a potential implementation for FEs, it is not a strict requirement. **Interoperability standards** are implied via the **entity-based design**, ensuring modular exchange of data and control signals between physical and digital components. No other standards (e.g., IEC 62264 for enterprise integration) are referenced.'}
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system must adhere to safety-critical requirements as referenced in ISO 15622:2018 for **Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) systems**, which specifies performance requirements and test procedures for intelligent transport systems. The system shall implement ACC functionalities as outlined in this standard, ensuring compliance with safety-critical domain constraints.

Additionally, the Digital Twin system must align with **V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything) service requirements** as defined in **3GPP TS 22.185**, which outlines technical specifications for secure and reliable communication protocols in vehicular networks. While the documents do not explicitly detail **ISO 26262-1:2018** or **ASIL (Automotive Safety Integrity Level)** methodologies, they imply adherence to broader **functional safety** principles for critical systems.

No explicit mention of **cybersecurity protocols**, **access control mechanisms**, **authentication systems**, or **threat mitigation strategies** is provided in the documents. The system's safety considerations are implicitly tied to **ISO 15622** and **3GPP TS 22.185**, which govern performance and communication standards rather than detailed security or safety engineering frameworks.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction is highly specific and largely accurate, identifying the 1/10-scale autonomous vehicle and its hardware components. It correctly captures the use of NVIDIA Jetson, ROS2, and the specific sensors (LiDAR, Depth Camera). However, it incorrectly adds 'radar' to the sensor list and mislabels the domain as 'cyber-biophysical' rather than 'cyber-physical' found in the text. Despite these minor inaccuracies, it is strongly grounded in the provided sources.

While the extraction correctly identifies the core services for the specific ACC case study—Monitoring and Control/Remote Management—it provides extensive, unsupported details for other domains (manufacturing, aerospace, battery) that are only briefly cited in the text. block_2_docs_retrieved:

offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a.
